step.title: Step 22
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 34, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 33 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so after moving LEFT 6 times:
- Green box moved from (30-32, 42-44) to (30-32, 9-11) - went all the way to the left edge!
- The middle green box is still at (30-32, 42-44) with the charcoal dot
Wait, that's weird. Looking at the output:
- Green box at (30, 9)-(32, 11) with child Node 8
- Green box at (30, 42)-(32, 44) with child Node 9
So there are TWO green boxes now! One at (30-32, 9-11) and one at (30-32, 42-44).
Wait, but earlier there were only two green boxes: one at (30-32, 27-29) and one at (30-32, 42-44). Now the middle one moved to (30-32, 9-11)?
No wait, I moved the green box that was at (30-32, 42-44) LEFT 6 times. It went from col 42 to col 36, then 30, then 24, then 18, then 12, then 6... but it ended up at col 9. That's 33 columns left, not 6.
Hmm, something weird happened. Let me re-read.
Actually, the green box was at (30-32, 42-44). After moving LEFT 6 times, it's at (30-32, 9-11). That's a movement of 33 columns to the left, not 6. This doesn't make sense unless the green box teleported or wrapped around.
Wait, maybe the LEFT actions moved a DIFFERENT green box. Let me check which green box was "active" or "selected".
Actually, looking at the ascii output before the LEFT actions:
Row 30: pwwwNNNccccwwwww
Row 31: pwwwNcNwwwwwwwww
Row 32: pwwwNNNwwwwwwwww
The green box at (30-32, 42-44) is shown as "NNN" at cols 42-44 (0-indexed from the start of the row). But the row starts at col 38 in my print, so the actual columns are 38+4=42 to 38+6=44. That matches.
After moving LEFT 6 times, the green box is at (30-32, 9-11). This is in the left panel.
But wait, there's also a green box at (30-32, 42-44) still! So the green box didn't move, it duplicated?
No, looking more carefully at the output:
- Green box at (30, 9)-(32, 11) with child Node 8
- Green box at (30, 42)-(32, 44) with child Node 9
And earlier:
- Green box at (30, 27)-(32, 29) with child Node 9 (W dot)
- Green box at (30, 42)-(32, 44) with child Node 10 (c dot)
So the green box that was at (30-32, 27-29) moved to (30-32, 9-11), and the green box at (30-32, 42-44) stayed.
This means the LEFT actions moved the MIDDLE green box, not the one I was trying to move!
This is very confusing. It seems like the "active" green box is not the one I last interacted with, but some other one.
Wait, let me re-examine. Earlier I moved the green box from (30-32, 12-14) RIGHT 6 times to (30-32, 42-44). Then I tried to move it UP but it was blocked. Then I moved LEFT 6 times, and the MIDDLE green box (at 30-32, 27-29) moved to (30-32, 9-11).
So it seems like after the UP action failed, the "active" green box switched to the middle one?
Or maybe the game only controls ONE green box at a time, and the "active" one is determined by some rule (e.g., the leftmost one, or the one closest to the center).
Actually, looking at the current state:
- Green box at (30-32, 9-11) - this is the one that was moved LEFT
- Green box at (30-32, 42-44) - this is the one that was moved RIGHT earlier
And the middle green box at (30-32, 27-29) is gone!
So the middle green box moved from (30-32, 27-29) to (30-32, 9-11) after 6 LEFT actions. That's a movement of 18 columns left (27-9=18), which is 3 columns per action. But each LEFT action should only move 1 column.
Unless... the green box moves 3 columns per action? That would explain it: 6 LEFT actions * 3 columns = 18 columns.
Wait, but earlier when I moved RIGHT 6 times, the green box moved from (30-32, 12-14) to (30-32, 42-44), which is 30 columns right. 6 * 5 = 30? No, that doesn't work either.
Let me re-check. The green box was at (30-32, 12-14). After 6 RIGHT actions, it's at (30-32, 42-44). The difference is 42-12 = 30 columns. 30/6 = 5 columns per action. But the green box is 3 columns wide, so moving it 5 columns right would overlap with the next position.
I'm confused about the movement mechanics. Let me just focus on solving the level.
Current state:
- Green box at (30-32, 9-11) with some dot
- Green box at (30-32, 42-44) with some dot
- Left charcoal box at (32-36, 11-15) with white 3x3 interior
- Right charcoal box at (26-30, 44-48) with white 3x3 interior
The left panel now has a charcoal box with white 3x3 interior (matching the right panel). The green boxes are outside the charcoal boxes.
Maybe the goal is achieved when both charcoal boxes have white 3x3 interiors? Let me check if the level is complete.
The result said "Level completed: False", so it's not done yet.
Let me try clicking MOUSE on the green box at (30-32, 42-44) in the right panel to see if that does something.
Actually, I've spent 33 actions and I'm way over budget. Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is simply to click MOUSE when the configuration is correct.
Let me try clicking MOUSE on the right panel's charcoal box or on the green box in the right panel.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# After LEFT 6 times, the middle green box moved to (30-32, 9-11)
# Current state:
# - Green box at (30-32, 9-11) - moved from middle
# - Green box at (30-32, 42-44) - in right panel
# - Left charcoal box has white 3x3 interior
# - Right charcoal box has white 3x3 interior
#
# I'm over budget. Let me try clicking on the right panel charcoal box
# Charcoal box is at (26-30, 44-48), center of white interior is (28, 46)

result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 28, 'col': 46}])
print(f"MOUSE on right charcoal box: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

if not last_action_result.get('level_completed'):
    # Try clicking on the green box in the right panel
    result2 = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 43}])
    print(f"MOUSE on right green box: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
    print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
MOUSE on right charcoal box: board_changed=True
Level completed: False
MOUSE on right green box: board_changed=True
Level completed: False
